best_image_source: Esri World Imagery aerial photo dated 11/13/2025; closest reference comparison is the provided ballasted roof reference for 4201 E 72nd Ave

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Large tan/brown aggregate field covers the main roof and attached rounded sections with a continuous granular texture and irregular stone pattern; the surface reads as loose ballast rather than a smooth single-ply sheet.
The roof also shows a fine, dense aggregate appearance across broad areas, which can resemble gravel-surfaced BUR from aerial view; the underlying assembly cannot be confirmed visually.
Some smaller attached light-toned roof areas appear smoother than the main aggregate field, but the imagery does not clearly show coating-specific application variation or exposed substrate.
The smaller light smooth-looking roof pieces could be a white/light single-ply membrane, but no seam grid or welded details are resolved well enough to confirm.

roof_age_estimate: Appears mature/older in aerial appearance; exact age unknown. The 11/13/2025 imagery shows weathered aggregate and multiple rooftop penetrations, but age cannot be determined from imagery alone.

notes: Broad roof-wide color variation and darker patches within the aggregate field may indicate weathering, retained moisture, staining, or localized repairs.
Numerous rooftop units, vents, curbs, and flashed penetrations increase leak risk on a low-slope assembly.
Trees overhang portions of the roof edge, creating debris and maintenance risk.
No clear standing water is resolved, but drainage performance cannot be verified from the aerial angle.

observations: Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, high penetration or rooftop-unit density, tree overhang or roof debris exposure.
Main roof field is a continuous tan/brown aggregate-surfaced low-slope assembly.

Several attached smaller roof sections are visible, including a rounded southern/western addition and smaller connector areas, and they appear to share the same aggregate surface or transition into smoother low-slope material.
Rooftop equipment, vents, and curbs are numerous across the roof, creating multiple flashing and seal points.
Perimeter and transition lines are visible, but seam or lap detail is not sufficiently resolved to confirm a specific hidden membrane beneath the aggregate.
summary: Esri World Imagery dated 11/13/2025 shows a commercial government building with a predominantly ballasted or gravel-surfaced low-slope roof and smaller attached roof sections of similar appearance. Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, high penetration or rooftop-unit density, tree overhang or roof debris exposure.
recommendation: Based on the visible aerial evidence, this roof should be treated as a mixed low-slope assembly with a dominant aggregate-surfaced roof and smaller attached sections that may be membrane or coated membrane. A qualified commercial roof-coating contractor should perform an onsite inspection, moisture testing where warranted, confirmation of the visible roof system and warranty eligibility, required repairs and preparation, and a reliable project-price proposal. If the aggregate-surfaced areas are structurally sound and moisture is not widespread, evaluate whether a silicone roof restoration system could be a cost-effective option before overlay or replacement; a qualifying silicone system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, improve resistance to UV exposure and hail, withstand ponding water when properly designed and installed, and qualify for a manufacturer’s 0

roof_zones.location: Dominant central and north roof field plus broad primary low-slope surface
supporting_cues: Continuous tan/brown granular field across the largest visible roof area
Irregular stone-like texture with three-dimensional mottling rather than a smooth sheet surface
Roof boundary follows an intentional, contiguous low-slope roof outline
No visible rigid metal panel geometry or resolved sheet-lap grid dominates the zone
limitations: Individual stones are only partly resolved from aerial height
Hidden membrane type cannot be determined
Some darker areas may reflect shadow, aging, or localized moisture

roof_zones.location: Southern rounded addition and adjacent smaller attached low-slope section
supporting_cues: This attached section appears to share the same granular surfacing as the main roof
Rounded geometry still shows a continuous aggregate texture across the visible field
Material transition is not distinct enough to support a different primary system
limitations: Small area with partial obstruction and limited seam detail
A smooth coated or membrane surface cannot be fully excluded at the smallest attached areas

roof_zones.location: East-side connector/canopy and smaller attached roof appendages
supporting_cues: Light-toned smooth-looking low-slope pieces are visible in the attachment area
No exposed aggregate texture is as clear as on the primary field
Could represent a different small roof field from the dominant aggregate assembly
limitations: No resolved seams, welds, or manufacturer details
Roof components and shadows obscure the material boundary

roof_zones.location: Main large central roof field and curved north roof field

candidates.evidence: Large smooth light tan/beige low-slope field with broad unbroken appearance; no resolved sheet seams or panel ribs. The color and evenness could reflect a coated roof, but membrane chemistry cannot be confirmed from the aerial view.
Light-colored smooth low-slope roof surface that could be a white or light membrane viewed in warm lighting; however seams are not resolved, so this is only a conservative fallback.
Uniform light membrane-like appearance is also consistent with PVC, but there is no PVC-specific evidence such as markings or clearly resolved thermoplastic details.

limitations: Strong sun/shadow and image resolution prevent separation of coating versus single-ply membrane.
No reliable seam or flash evidence is visible across most of the field.
Color alone is insufficient to establish exact membrane chemistry.

roof_zones.location: Southwest rounded addition and adjacent lower roof extension

candidates.evidence: This attached roof section has the same smooth light tan field as the main roof, with no visible granular texture or rigid metal panel geometry. A reflective or aging coating remains plausible.
The zone appears as a smooth light membrane-like low-slope surface, so TPO is a conservative default if it is a single-ply roof.
The visible detail is insufficient to distinguish a membrane from a coated surface with confidence.

limitations: Small attachments are visible but not enough detail to resolve material family precisely.
Rounded geometry and shadowing obscure surface cues.
No clear lap grid, aggregate, or metal ribs are visible.

roof_zones.location: Small right-side connector/canopy and minor roof appendages along the east edge

candidates.evidence: Small light-toned low-slope roof pieces appear smooth and membrane-like, which is most conservatively treated as TPO when exact chemistry cannot be separated.
The same light finish could also be a coated surface; lack of resolved seams or substrate clues keeps coating in the running.
The appendage is small and partially obstructed, so the surface type cannot be established responsibly.

limitations: Very small visible area and rooftop objects limit surface interpretation.
Potential parapets, flashings, and equipment pads complicate the view.
No clear evidence for metal, ballast, or asphaltic surfacing is present.

overall_limitations: No zone shows clear metal ribs, ballast stone, or aggregate-surfaced BUR.
The roof appears to be mostly smooth light low-slope surfacing, but the imagery does not support exact membrane chemistry.
Estimated area percentages are approximate and based on visible roof plan rather than measured footprint.
